findings.title: capex-trend silently produces value=0 when capex array entry is null
findings.description: capexT and capexT3 are computed with `Math.abs(typeof capexArr[0] === 'object' && capexArr[0] !== null ? capexArr[0].value : capexArr[0])`. When `capexArr[0]` is `null` (a common cache-gap pattern), `typeof null === 'object'` is TRUE but the `!== null` guard fails, so the ternary evaluates to `capexArr[0]` itself, which is `null`. Math.abs(null) returns 0 (not NaN). The downstream null-check on line 25 (`capexT == null`) returns false because `capexT === 0`. The ratio computation proceeds with capexT=0, value becomes 0, and `value <= 1.5` passes — silently treating a stock with no capex data as having zero capex.
findings.mechanism: Math.abs(null) === 0. The guard `capexT == null` only catches null/undefined, not 0. When the raw input is null, capexT collapses to 0 and the method falsely passes.
findings.reproducer: annual.annualCapex = [null, {value: 5e9}, {value: 4e9}, {value: 3e9}]; revs >= 4. capexT becomes 0; ratioT=0; value=0/(non-zero)=0; pass=true.
findings.impact: Stocks with missing latest-year capex data silently pass this DIAGNOSTIC, distorting scoring breakdowns. The same pattern also affects capexT3 (index 3) when the 3-year-ago entry is null.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the unsafe ternary with explicit unwrap+nullcheck: `const _c0 = capexArr[0]; const c0v = (_c0 == null) ? null : (typeof _c0 === 'number' ? _c0 : _c0.value); if (c0v == null) return incomputable; capexT = Math.abs(c0v);`

findings.title: sbc-revenue does not Math.abs(sbc), inverting pass on negative-stored SBC
findings.description: fcf-yield.js (per Bug #16 fix) uses `Math.abs(sbc)` because Yahoo stores SBC with inconsistent sign (sometimes negative when reported as cash-flow outflow). sbc-revenue.js does NOT apply Math.abs and computes `value = sbc / rev` directly. When SBC is stored negative (e.g. -2.5B), value becomes negative (e.g. -0.10). The pass test `value <= 0.15` is TRUE for any negative number — silent false-pass.
findings.mechanism: sbc-revenue uses the raw value; fcf-yield was fixed to Math.abs(sbc). The two methods now have inconsistent sign treatment of the same field. A company with -25% SBC/Revenue (heavy dilution) passes <= 15% sanity check.
findings.reproducer: annual.annualSBC = [{value: -2500e6}], annual.annualRev = [{value: 10000e6}]. value = -0.25; pass = (-0.25 <= 0.15) = true.
findings.impact: Hypergrowth SaaS stocks with heavy dilution silently pass the SBC sanity check, defeating the purpose of the method. Inconsistent with fcf-yield behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Apply `Math.abs(sbc)` consistently: `const value = Math.abs(sbc) / rev;`

findings.title: quarterly-earnings-stability scales threshold with Math.ceil — demands 100% pass at small windows
findings.description: `const scaled = Math.ceil(THRESHOLD * window.length / 8)` — for window.length=4 → ceil(6*4/8)=ceil(3)=3 (requires 3/4=75%, OK). For window.length=5 → ceil(3.75)=4 (requires 4/5=80%, harsher than the intended 6/8=75%). For window.length=6 → ceil(4.5)=5 (5/6=83%). For window.length=7 → ceil(5.25)=6 (6/7=86%). The intended scaling ratio is 75%, but ceil pushes it above. This is exactly the bug pattern fixed in piotroski-f-score (Tag 174 Bug #19, ceil→round).
findings.mechanism: Math.ceil over a fractional threshold systematically raises the required pass count. A stock with 5 quarters and 4 positive (80%) fails; same stock with 8 quarters and 6 positive (75%) passes.
findings.reproducer: 8Q netIncomeQ where positions 0-4 exist (5 quarters available), 4 are positive. scaledThreshold=4, positive=4, pass=true (boundary). But 7Q with 5 positive (71%) → scaledThreshold=ceil(5.25)=6 → fail despite being above the intended 75% ratio.
findings.impact: Stocks with 5-7 quarters of data are evaluated harsher than 8-quarter stocks. Discriminates against newer IPOs and companies with reporting gaps.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `Math.ceil` with `Math.max(1, Math.round(THRESHOLD * window.length / 8))` — same pattern as the piotroski fix.

findings.title: quarterly-revenue-acceleration treats plain-number quarterly entries as missing
findings.description: `const latest = ts[0] && ts[0].value` — this only works when ts[0] is an object `{value: x}`. When ts[0] is a plain number (the parallel methods e.g. stable-quarterly-growth, hypergrowth-quality-class explicitly handle both forms via `typeof === 'number'`), `(7e9).value === undefined`, so latest=undefined, then `latest == null` is true → incomputable. The snapshot envelope shape is heterogeneous across the pipeline.
findings.mechanism: Object-envelope assumption: this method silently treats the entire timeseries as missing when the upstream stores quarters as plain numbers. Other methods accept both shapes.
findings.reproducer: timeseries.revenueQ = [7e9, 6.5e9, 6e9, 5.5e9]. ts[0] && ts[0].value → 7e9 && undefined → undefined. latest=undefined, incomputable.
findings.impact: Snapshot envelope inconsistency causes false incomputables for this DIAGNOSTIC. Affects reporting of QoQ acceleration when quarterly data is stored as plain numbers.
findings.suggested_fix: Use the same helper as elsewhere: `const _v = q => q == null ? null : (typeof q === 'number' ? q : q.value); const latest = _v(ts[0]); const prev = _v(ts[1]);`

findings.title: working-capital-anomaly assumes annualRev entries are always {value:...} objects
findings.description: `const revT = revs[0] && revs[0].value; const revT1 = revs[1] && revs[1].value;` — if upstream stores revs as plain numbers (some method-files explicitly support this form), revT is undefined, then the null check on line 32 marks incomputable. The wcProxy reads `b.totalAssets` from annualBalance entries (always objects) which is fine, but the revenue access pattern is inconsistent with e.g. profitability-state._getArr which handles both.
findings.mechanism: Object-envelope assumption: if annualRev is stored as `[1e10, 9e9]` (plain numbers) the method returns 'missing values' even though revenue data is present.
findings.reproducer: annual.annualRev = [12e9, 10e9], annualBalance with valid totalAssets/totalDebt/totalCash → revT=undefined → incomputable.
findings.impact: Silent incomputable on stocks where the snapshot stores annualRev as plain numbers. Method appears 'no data' rather than computing a real value.
findings.suggested_fix: Use the canonical unwrap pattern: `const revT = revs[0] == null ? null : (typeof revs[0] === 'number' ? revs[0] : revs[0].value);` — and likewise for revT1.

findings.title: forecast-contamination-guard, quarter-concentration-guard sum filtered Q-array as if positionally aligned
findings.description: Both methods use `_arrVals` which filters out null/non-finite quarters BEFORE indexing. forecast-contamination-guard computes `q4Sum = qRev[0] + qRev[1] + qRev[2] + qRev[3]`, intending the trailing 4 quarters. quarter-concentration-guard does `qRev.slice(0, 4)`. When quarterly data has cache gaps (a frequent reality — Yahoo sometimes returns only 3 of last 4 quarters), the filtered array shifts older quarters into positions 0-3, and the 'TTM' sum/share is computed from a non-contiguous mix of e.g. last quarter + 3 quarters from 12-18 months ago. The annual vs Q-Sum ratio then is meaningless — a false-positive 'forecast contamination' (or false negative when missing the latest Q deflates the sum).
findings.mechanism: Filtering before positional indexing collapses time-aligned series. The methods assume positions 0-3 = newest 4 calendar quarters, but with filtered arrays those positions just mean 'first 4 non-null entries'.
findings.reproducer: timeseries.revenueQ = [{value: 10e9}, null, {value: 8e9}, {value: 7e9}, {value: 6e9}]. After _arrVals: [10e9, 8e9, 7e9, 6e9]. q4Sum=31e9 but actually represents 5 calendar quarters of revenue minus a gap — annualRev[0]/q4Sum will be artificially low or high depending on direction.
findings.impact: Forecast-contamination guard can either falsely fire on legitimate annual data (gap shifts older Q forward, deflating q4Sum so ratio looks like 'inflated annual') or miss real Yahoo forecast contamination. Quarter-concentration guard similarly mis-measures the Q-share. This is a DATAGUARD that disqualifies stocks — a false fire kills a stock from the pick list.
findings.suggested_fix: Use the raw array preserving null positions (like q-spike-dataguard's `_rawArr`). Index 0-3 only when all four are finite; otherwise return incomputable. Pattern: `const qRaw = _rawArr(stock, 'timeseries.revenueQ'); if (qRaw.length < 4 || qRaw.slice(0,4).some(v => !Number.isFinite(v))) return incomputable;`

findings.title: q-spike-dataguard 3y-decline check indexes filtered annualRev as if positional
findings.description: `const revY = _arr(stock, 'annual.annualRev')` returns the filtered (non-null) values. Line 167 then does `if (revY.length >= 4)` and uses `revY[0]` as latest, `revY[3]` as 'threeYrAgo'. But if any of the most recent 4 annual revenue entries are null (a real cache-miss pattern, especially for non-US tickers), revY[3] is the 4th non-null entry, which can be 4-7 calendar years ago, not 3.
findings.mechanism: Same root cause as F-ME-006: filtered-array indexing assumes positional alignment that the filter destroys. The method then computes an annual-decline metric across a non-aligned window and may falsely flag (or miss) a cyclical-rebound pattern.
findings.reproducer: annualRev = [{value: 5e9}, null, {value: 4e9}, {value: 3e9}, {value: 7e9}]. revY (filtered) = [5e9, 4e9, 3e9, 7e9]. revY[3]=7e9 (actually 4 years ago, not 3). latest=5e9 < threeYrAgo=7e9 → fail = 'Cyclical-Rebound'. Reality: latest > actual-3y-ago=3e9 (no rebound pattern), so a false hard-fail.
findings.impact: DATAGUARD false-fires for stocks with a single null year in their annual revenue history, disqualifying them from Hypergrowth picks. Particularly affects international stocks where Yahoo cache misses are common.
findings.suggested_fix: Switch to raw (positionally-aligned) array via the existing `_rawArr` helper and require `Number.isFinite(revY[0]) && Number.isFinite(revY[3])` before computing the decline.

findings.title: altman-z-score X4 understates total liabilities by using totalDebt only
findings.description: X4 in Altman Z″ is Book Value of Equity / Total Liabilities. The implementation uses `bookEquity = assets - debtVal` (just totalDebt) and divides by `Math.max(debtVal, assets*0.01)`. Total liabilities includes accounts payable, deferred revenue, lease obligations, accrued expenses — for many companies these dwarf interest-bearing debt. Using only totalDebt overstates equity AND understates the denominator, inflating X4 by a factor of 2-5x for typical companies. The 1.05 weight on X4 plus the inflation pushes many companies above the 1.1 distress threshold that shouldn't be.
findings.mechanism: Yahoo's annualBalance has totalDebt (financial debt) but not totalLiabilities directly. Using totalDebt as proxy makes X4 systematically biased upward, especially for capital-light businesses (SaaS with large deferred-revenue balances appear safer than they are; biotech with large accrued-R&D liabilities likewise).
findings.reproducer: A SaaS company with totalAssets=1e9, totalDebt=100M, but unbilled deferred revenue + accrued liabilities = 400M (real equity = 500M, real total-liabilities = 500M). Current code: bookEquity = 900M, X4 = 900M / 100M = 9.0 → contributes 9.45 to Z″. Correct: bookEquity = 500M, X4 = 500M / 500M = 1.0 → contributes 1.05 to Z″. Difference of ~8 points in Z-score.
findings.impact: TURNAROUND-mode passing stocks that are actually in distress. The threshold (1.1) was calibrated against the real Altman model — at the current X4 implementation, the 'distress zone' is essentially unreachable for many balance-sheet structures.
findings.suggested_fix: If a totalLiabilities field becomes available in the snapshot, use it. Until then, document the bias and either skip X4 (treat as 0) for fairness or use `(assets - totalDebt*K)` with K calibrated empirically. A safer interim is to drop X4 entirely (set its coefficient to 0) and rescale the threshold downward.

findings.title: PEG falls through to revenueGrowth when earningsGrowth is exactly 0
findings.description: `const earningsGrowth = H.metricValue(stock, 'earningsGrowthYoY') || H.metricValue(stock, 'epsGrowthYoY');` — if earningsGrowthYoY is 0 (a legitimate value: flat earnings), `||` treats 0 as falsy and falls through to epsGrowthYoY, then to revenueGrowthYoY. The comment 'Bug #4 fix: prefer earnings growth' is undermined: zero-earnings-growth stocks silently get evaluated against revenue growth, defeating the stated Lynch PEG logic.
findings.mechanism: Classic `||` operator-precedence bug: distinguishing 'missing' from '0' requires explicit null check. Lynch PEG = PE/EPS-growth; using revenue growth on stocks with zero EPS growth produces artificially attractive PEG values.
findings.reproducer: metrics.earningsGrowthYoY = {value: 0}, metrics.epsGrowthYoY null, metrics.revenueGrowthYoY = {value: 25}. growth becomes 25 (rev), source labeled 'Rev' silently. PE/25 looks attractive vs the real PE/0 (incomputable).
findings.impact: Stocks with stalled earnings but growing revenue silently get attractive PEG scores, surfacing in TURNAROUND/HYPERGROWTH sort by PEG. Misleads Karl's deep-dive selection.
findings.suggested_fix: Use explicit null check: `let earningsGrowth = H.metricValue(stock, 'earningsGrowthYoY'); if (earningsGrowth == null) earningsGrowth = H.metricValue(stock, 'epsGrowthYoY');`

findings.title: net-debt-ebitda treats EBITDA<=0 as incomputable, hiding distressed stocks
findings.description: When EBITDA (= OpInc * 1.2) is <= 0 (operating loss), the method returns `computable: false`. As a DATAGUARD, this means a stock with negative operating income but high debt is treated as 'no data' rather than 'failed solvency check'. The downstream `isDisqualifiedByDataGuards` only fires when `computable === true && pass === false`, so this DATAGUARD silently passes the most distressed companies.
findings.mechanism: A company with debt-to-near-zero-EBITDA ratio is effectively infinite leverage. Treating it as incomputable allows the stock to skip the hard-fail gate while paradoxically being the highest-risk profile.
findings.reproducer: annual.annualOpInc[0] = -500M (operating loss), totalDebt = 2B, totalCash = 100M. EBITDA = -600M < 0 → computable=false → DATAGUARD does NOT disqualify. Stock proceeds through hygiene layer into picks for HYPERGROWTH/TURNAROUND modes.
findings.impact: Distressed companies bypass the net-debt-ebitda DATAGUARD. Tag 174 fixes addressed similar 'silent-pass' patterns; this is the same pattern still live for the most-at-risk stocks.
findings.suggested_fix: When EBITDA <= 0 AND netDebt > 0, return `computable: true, pass: false, value: Infinity` (with a special flag like 'NEGATIVE_EBITDA_WITH_DEBT'). When EBITDA <= 0 AND netDebt <= 0 (net-cash with operating loss), incomputable is reasonable.

findings.title: premium-compounder-proof c4 silently substitutes totalCash=0 when missing
findings.description: Check #4 (Net Cash OR ND/EBITDA <= 1) uses `(totalCash || 0) - totalDebt`. When totalCash is null/undefined (missing in snapshot), it's treated as 0. For a cash-rich company with a missing totalCash field, netCash is computed as `-totalDebt` (negative), failing the net-cash test. The ND/EBITDA fallback also uses `(totalCash || 0)`, again understating cash. A real net-cash company with a missing cash field silently fails the premium-compounder proof.
findings.mechanism: Silent `|| 0` fallback hides missing data as 'zero cash'. The check is conservatively wrong: it falsely fails companies whose totalCash is missing rather than marking the check incomputable.
findings.reproducer: annualBalance[0] = {totalAssets: 100B, totalDebt: 5B, totalCash: null}. (totalCash || 0) = 0. netCash = -5B → fails 'netCash >= 0'. ND/EBITDA = 5B / (OpInc*1.2). If small EBITDA, fails <=1 test. Check #4 fails.
findings.impact: Premium-compounder-proof falsely fails on net-cash companies with cache gaps in totalCash. The premium-compounder is a HIGH-impact gate (allows conditional FCF-yield 1.5-3% to pass), so this directly affects which top-tier compounders surface.
findings.suggested_fix: Mark check #4 incomputable when totalCash is missing (cannot evaluate net-cash). Pattern: `const c4Computable = totalDebt != null && totalCash != null;`

findings.title: profitability-trend classifies y0=0 with y1>0 as DETERIORATING
findings.description: Sign-flip branch: `if (y0 <= 0 && y1 > 0) return 'DETERIORATING'`. The <= boundary means y0=0 (exact breakeven year after a profitable prior year) is classified as DETERIORATING rather than FLAT/marginal-decline. While breakeven-from-profit is a deterioration, the cliff is severe: a company with y1=10M, y0=0 (flat-to-zero) gets the same harsh label as one with y1=10M, y0=-100M. The 'same-sign positive' branch below would handle this more proportionally if y0>0.
findings.mechanism: <= boundary in a sign-flip rule treats a marginal endpoint (0) as if it had flipped sign. Companies sitting exactly at breakeven get a DETERIORATING tag in their trend.
findings.reproducer: annual.annualNetIncome = [0, 5e8]. y0=0, y1=5e8. Hits `y0 <= 0 && y1 > 0` → DETERIORATING. Same result as if y0 had been -5e8.
findings.impact: Trend direction misclassification for breakeven year stocks. Affects 2-axis matrix display ('Stable × Deteriorating') and the profitability-trend pass test for TURNAROUND-mode picks.
findings.suggested_fix: Tighten boundary to strict less-than: `if (y0 < 0 && y1 > 0) return 'DETERIORATING';` then fall through to a y0==0 explicit branch (return FLAT if y1 small, DETERIORATING if y1 large).

findings.title: modes-report dqGrade='A+' produces invalid CSS class cb-dq-a+
findings.description: data-quality.js can return grade 'A+' (best tier). In renderCard: `dqHtml = `${dqGrade}`` produces class 'cb-dq-a+'. The '+' is not a valid CSS class character — in HTML it parses, but CSS selectors interpret 'cb-dq-a+' as 'cb-dq-a' adjacent-sibling. The CSS file only defines `.cb-dq-a`, `.cb-dq-b`, `.cb-dq-c`, `.cb-dq-d` — A+ stocks fall back to inherited styling (likely no background color), making the A+ badge visually indistinguishable from A or default.
findings.mechanism: Schema drift between data-quality.js (writes 'A+') and generate-modes-report.js CSS palette (only A/B/C/D). The class with '+' parses but doesn't match any rule.
findings.reproducer: stock._quality = {grade: 'A+'}. Generated HTML: `A+`. Browser inspector shows no matched .cb-dq-a+ rule.
findings.impact: Cosmetic but obscures the A+ tier from the user. Karl can't tell A+ from missing-grade at a glance.
findings.suggested_fix: Either map A+→'aplus' for the class name: `dqGrade.toLowerCase().replace('+','plus')`, OR add a `.cb-dq-aplus` CSS rule. Best: change data-quality.js to emit 'APLUS' as the grade name, or normalize all grades to a single token.

findings.title: score-aggregator lte_abs ratio uses 1e-10 floor producing 0.99 for value=0
findings.description: For lte_abs methods (e.g. sloan-ratio with WARN_THRESHOLD=0.10), when val=0 (perfect, no Sloan accruals): absVal=0; `ratio = threshold / Math.max(0, 1e-10) = 0.10 / 1e-10 = 1e9`. Then `ratio >= 0.9` matches → returns `Math.min(0.99, 0.7 + (1e9 - 0.9) * 2.9)` = 0.99. But a perfect Sloan score should actually be PASS (val=0 satisfies |0|<=0.10), and would hit the `pass=true` early-return on line 84 (return 1.0). So the lte_abs branch is only reached when pass is already false (the method threshold itself was exceeded). In that case, the scaling is intended for fail-but-near-threshold — but the floor of 1e-10 creates a huge inflated ratio when absVal is very small near zero. The graduation curve treats val=0.0001 as nearly-perfect failing pass.
findings.mechanism: The 1e-10 floor was added to prevent division-by-zero, but for lte_abs the natural scaling should be `min(1, threshold/absVal)` clipped — instead the unclipped ratio passes into the graduation curve at exponential scale, only saved by the `Math.min(0.99, ...)` clamp. The curve `0.7 + (ratio-0.9)*2.9` is sensitive to small ratio inputs above 0.9 but the curve range is mis-designed for huge ratios.
findings.reproducer: Sloan = 0.105 (just over WARN_THRESHOLD=0.10), pass=false (let's say). threshold=0.10, absVal=0.105, ratio=0.10/0.105=0.95. Graduation: 0.7+(0.95-0.9)*2.9 = 0.845. Reasonable. But if val=0.011 (just over 10% but barely), threshold=0.10, ratio=0.10/0.011=9.09 → clamped to 0.99. Compared to val=0.105 → 0.845. The 'closer to threshold' val=0.011 scores LESS than val=0.105 — inverted.
findings.impact: lte_abs methods produce non-monotonic normalized scores. A worse-than-threshold value can score higher than a barely-worse-than-threshold value when both are near zero. Affects sloan-ratio scoring weight in mode aggregation.
findings.suggested_fix: For lte_abs, when pass=false, compute `ratio = absVal / threshold` (how-much-over), then map: ratio in [1,2] → linear graduation 0.6→0.3; ratio >2 → 0.1. This is monotone (higher absVal → lower score). Current code has the direction inverted.

findings.title: earnings-stability oiNeeded scales with available data, ignoring required minimum
findings.description: `const oiNeeded = Math.min(4, opIncWindow.length); const fcfNeeded = Math.min(4, fcfWindow.length);` — for a stock with only 4 years of data, oiNeeded=4 (100% positive required). For 5 years, oiNeeded=4 (80% positive). The method's stated docstring requires '4/5 years positive' = 80%. A stock with 4y history of [+, +, +, -] (75%) has oiPositive=3, oiNeeded=4 → fail. Same stock with 5y history [+, +, +, -, +] (80%) has oiPositive=4, oiNeeded=4 → pass. The cliff at exactly 4 vs 5 years means companies with one missing year are evaluated more strictly than companies with five years of data.
findings.mechanism: The 'need at least 4 positive' rule was designed for the 5-year window. Truncating to length-of-window without rescaling the threshold creates an inconsistent bar.
findings.reproducer: annual.annualOpInc = [{value: 100}, {value: 80}, {value: 60}, {value: -10}] (4 years, one loss year, 75% positive). oiNeeded=min(4,4)=4, oiPositive=3 → fail. Same company with 5 years [+, +, +, -, +] (80% positive) → oiNeeded=4, oiPositive=4 → pass.
findings.impact: Stocks with shorter history are evaluated more strictly. Especially affects newer-IPO Quality-Compounder candidates with exactly 4 years of public financials.
findings.suggested_fix: Scale needed proportionally: `const oiNeeded = Math.max(3, Math.round(opIncWindow.length * 0.8));` — keeps the 80% bar consistent. Or document that 4y minimum needs ALL 4 positive (current behavior) and 5y needs 4/5.

findings.title: score-aggregator computedWeight check off-by-one allows single-method pass to bypass coverage gate
findings.description: The insufficient-coverage gate is `computedWeight / totalWeight < 0.4`. For HYPERGROWTH (totalWeight=1.0 sum across 6 methods), only 40% of weight must be computable. profitability-state has weight 0.15 + hypergrowth-quality-class 0.15 + revenue-growth-3y 0.25 = 0.55 → passes the 40% gate even if rule-of-40, gross-margin-stability, and rule-of-x are all incomputable. The resulting score is computed from 3 of 6 inputs — a 'majority-of-weight-is-missing' score is being reported as a real number. Calling this REJECT-with-reason 'insufficient-coverage' would be safer.
findings.mechanism: The 40% gate is permissive — half the methods can be incomputable and the score still publishes.
findings.reproducer: HYPERGROWTH mode, stock with revenue-growth-3y computable, hypergrowth-quality-class computable, profitability-state computable; rule-of-40, rule-of-x, gross-margin-stability all incomputable. computedWeight=0.55, totalWeight=1.0, ratio=0.55 ≥ 0.4 → score published from 55% of the intended inputs.
findings.impact: Stocks with sparse data still get a score that looks comparable to stocks with full data. The tier classification (A/B/NEAR_MISS) becomes noise for low-coverage stocks. The dataQuality grade-cap mitigates this only when DATAQUALITY_ENFORCE=1 (default off).
findings.suggested_fix: Raise the threshold to 0.7 (require 70% of weight computable), OR weight the score upward by `1 / (computedWeight/totalWeight)` as a confidence-adjusted output. Surface the computedWeight/totalWeight ratio in the breakdown for UI transparency.

findings.title: sector-medians-compute regional bucket uses threshold MIN_STOCKS_PER_REGION_SECTOR=20, falls through silently for small regions
findings.description: Per-region medians require ≥20 stocks. For smaller regions (e.g. EM = Brazil + India + Mexico + South Africa combined) and small subprofiles, the regional bucket is omitted entirely. The fallback to _GLOBAL means an EM stock gets evaluated against the global median (dominated by US stocks). This is the documented design, but the sample-count gate is hard — there's no graceful degradation (e.g. 'use regional median with low-confidence flag if n>=10'). For sub-profiles like INSURANCE in APAC, this likely means EVERY APAC stock is evaluated against a global insurance median calibrated mostly on US insurers.
findings.mechanism: Hard threshold (n>=20) plus no intermediate confidence tier. The regional calibration story is undermined because the regions that need it most (EM) usually fail the gate.
findings.reproducer: regionalBuckets.EM.RETAIL has 12 stocks. computeFromBuckets requires arr.length >= 20 → no EM/RETAIL median emitted. effectiveThreshold falls to _GLOBAL → which is dominated by US retail stocks.
findings.impact: The Tag 167 regional calibration story is partial: regions with insufficient sample counts silently fall back to a globally-US-dominated median, defeating the calibration intent for the very regions that need it.
findings.suggested_fix: Two-tier threshold: ≥20 = 'high-confidence regional', ≥10 = 'low-confidence regional' (emit with a flag). Adjust lookupMedian and effectiveThreshold to prefer high-confidence regional > low-confidence regional > _GLOBAL.

findings.title: revenue-volatility-guard treats unknown currency as USD without flag
findings.description: `toUsdRough` returns `value` unchanged when the currency code is not in FX_TO_USD. The comment claims 'conservatively treat as USD'. But for an unknown currency (e.g. ARS, NGN, VND), treating raw local-currency revenue as USD-equivalent dramatically misclassifies materiality. A Vietnam stock with 2B VND TTM revenue (~80k USD) is treated as 2B USD — way above the 100M material floor → guard activates when it shouldn't (the stock should be flagged as immaterial).
findings.mechanism: Silent fall-through for unknown currency codes. The 'conservatively USD' assumption is exactly backward for typical Yahoo coverage of EM tickers reporting in non-USD currencies.
findings.reproducer: stock.meta.reportingCurrency='VND', ttmRev (in VND) = 2e9 (≈ $80k USD). toUsdRough returns 2e9 (unchanged). 2e9 >= 100M → material → method evaluates as if real material company.
findings.impact: Small-cap EM stocks (reporting in unsupported currencies) trigger volatility checks designed for material companies. Either falsely passes (volatile but small, no concern) or falsely fails (small company with one bad year disqualified from HG).
findings.suggested_fix: When currency code is unknown, return `null` from toUsdRough → mark method incomputable with reason 'unknown reporting currency'. Add the missing currencies (ARS, NGN, VND, EGP, COP) to the static FX table.

findings.title: piotroski signal-6 (delta_liquidity) accepts missing assets1 silently
findings.description: The outer if-condition only requires cash0, cash1, debt0, debt1 to be non-null. assets1 may be null. The denominator computation uses `assets1 != null ? assets1 * 0.01 : assets0 * 0.01` — silently fall back to using assets0 (current year assets) for the prior-year denominator floor. This asymmetric fallback compares prior-year cash against current-year asset basis, which can flip the liq0 vs liq1 ordering and produce wrong signal direction.
findings.mechanism: Asymmetric fallback in the floor mechanism. When assets1 is missing, the floor for liq1 uses assets0, which may be larger (companies typically grow), making the floor higher and depressing liq1, falsely making liq0>liq1 → signal passes.
findings.reproducer: cash0=10, cash1=20 (cash actually decreased — bad), debt0=100, debt1=80, assets0=1000, assets1=null. denom0 = max(100, 10) = 100. denom1 = max(80, 10) = 80 (using assets0 as floor). liq0 = 10/100 = 0.1. liq1 = 20/80 = 0.25. liq0 > liq1 = false → signal fails (correctly). But if cash actually increased and debt grew proportionally, the asymmetric assets1 fallback can shift the comparison.
findings.impact: Edge-case signal noise on stocks with missing prior-year balance entries. Low impact because the asymmetry typically points conservatively (signal fails).
findings.suggested_fix: Require assets1 in the outer if-condition: `if (cash0 != null && cash1 != null && debt0 != null && debt1 != null && assets1 != null && assets1 > 0)`. Drop the signal when prior-year assets are missing.

findings.title: sloan-ratio consecutiveHigh check uses sloans[k].year+1 but sloans entries can have non-contiguous years skipped
findings.description: The 2-year-rule check iterates `sloans` (filtered for valid year-pairs) and breaks if `sloans[k].year !== sloans[k-1].year + 1`. But the year index here is the original array position in rawNis (which is also the chronological position in `latest-first` order). So `year+1` means 'one year older' which is the next chronological year. Wait — actually since arr[0]=latest, arr[1]=prior year, arr[k].year is the array INDEX. So sloans[0].year=0 (latest), sloans[1].year=1 (prior year). The check `sloans[k].year !== sloans[k - 1].year + 1` is correct: year=0 → year=1 → year=2 etc. should be contiguous. If a year is skipped (e.g. only [0, 2, 3]), the gap is detected. Good. Then 'consecutiveHigh >= 2' fires only on two adjacent years. But the loop breaks immediately when a year ISN'T high — so consecutiveHigh counts only contiguous high-from-latest years. This is consistent with the docstring 'CHRONIC_FAIL = >20% in 2 consecutive years' but specifically requires the LATEST two years to both be high. A pattern like [high, normal, high, high] (latest is fine, but 2 of last 4 are high) escapes — possibly intended, but worth flagging.
findings.mechanism: consecutiveHigh only counts from the latest year forward. A stock that had two adjacent high-sloan years that aren't the latest two is not flagged. This may be intentional (focus on current-pattern), but the docstring says '2 aufeinanderfolgenden Jahren' (consecutive) without specifying 'including the latest'.
findings.reproducer: sloans (computed): year0=8%, year1=22%, year2=25%, year3=5%. Latest is below threshold (8% < 20%), so consecutiveHigh=0 → CHRONIC_FAIL not triggered. But years 1+2 are both above 20% — chronic per docstring.
findings.impact: Earnings-manipulation detector misses cases where the chronic period isn't the most-recent two years. A 'reformed' company that just had two good recent years after a chronic period passes the DATAGUARD.
findings.suggested_fix: Compute consecutiveHigh by iterating all sloans and tracking the longest contiguous run >FAIL_THRESHOLD, not just the leading run. `let longestRun=0, run=0; for (k=0;k<sloans.length;k++) { /* break gaps */ if (Math.abs(sloans[k].value)>FAIL) run++; else run=0; longestRun=Math.max(longestRun,run); } if (longestRun>=2) flag=CHRONIC_FAIL.`

findings.title: data-quality nanRatio>1 (D grade) is documented as 'impossible' but tierCapForGrade default returns null
findings.description: The grade-thresholds comment says 'D: > 100% = impossible with finite weights'. But the GRADE_THRESHOLDS object terminates at C=1.00 — there's no 'D' threshold. If nanRatio is exactly 1.0 (every field missing), grade=C (the `nanRatio <= 1.00` branch). To get grade='D' you'd need nanRatio > 1.0 which is mathematically impossible with positive weights and clean inputs. tierCapForGrade has a 'D'→'REJECT' case that is unreachable. Conversely, if snapshot is the empty object `{}`, line 91 returns `grade: 'D'` directly (special-case for invalid input). So D IS reachable via this side-channel.
findings.mechanism: Inconsistency between threshold computation (can't yield D) and direct emit (returns D for invalid snapshot). Code path looks complete but the 'C' tier silently swallows what should be 'D' for severely incomplete snapshots.
findings.reproducer: snapshot with ZERO critical fields present: nanRatio = 12.5/12.5 = 1.0 → grade='C' (per `<= 1.00`). But intuitively 100%-missing should be grade='D'. Only `gradeSnapshot(null)` returns 'D'.
findings.impact: Tier-cap enforcement (DATAQUALITY_ENFORCE=1) sees stocks with 100% missing critical fields as 'C' → NEAR_MISS cap, not 'D' → REJECT. They can still appear in picks at NEAR_MISS tier.
findings.suggested_fix: Either tighten C to nanRatio < 1.0 and route 1.0 → D, or remove the 'D' case from tierCapForGrade and document that the empty-snapshot guard returns D. Suggested: `if (nanRatio < 1.0) grade='C'; else grade='D';`

findings.title: score-aggregator data-quality cap downgrades but never upgrades or caps NEAR_MISS
findings.description: When DATAQUALITY_ENFORCE=1 and dataQuality.grade='C', tierCapForGrade returns 'NEAR_MISS'. The code applies the cap only when current tier is A or B: `if (cap === 'NEAR_MISS' && (tier === 'A' || tier === 'B'))`. But if the unmodified tier is 'REJECT', the cap doesn't fire and dataQualityCapped stays false. That's fine. However if the unmodified tier is already NEAR_MISS, dataQualityCapped is also false despite the cap being theoretically applied (no-op for same-tier). When UI surfaces 'dataQualityCapped' to indicate 'this stock's data quality limited its tier', NEAR_MISS stocks with grade C show no badge even though the cap would have applied if they'd been better.
findings.mechanism: The boolean dataQualityCapped is set only when an actual downgrade happened, not when the cap was the binding constraint. UI cannot distinguish 'cap was hit' from 'cap was not binding' for already-NEAR_MISS stocks.
findings.reproducer: stock with grade=C, computed score=55 (would be NEAR_MISS naturally). tierCapForGrade('C')='NEAR_MISS'. Code path: cap !== 'REJECT', cap === 'NEAR_MISS' but tier is already NEAR_MISS, so the `(tier === 'A' || tier === 'B')` clause fails → dataQualityCapped=false. UI shows no DQ-cap indicator.
findings.impact: Minor UI/transparency issue. Users see different DQ-cap indicators for stocks that share the same DQ-limited fate. Confusing in cross-stock comparisons.
findings.suggested_fix: Set dataQualityCapped=true whenever the cap is being honored (cap === current tier or stricter), not only when a downgrade occurred. Pattern: `const capRank = {A:3,B:2,NEAR_MISS:1,REJECT:0}; if (capRank[cap] < capRank[tier]) { dataQualityCapped=true; tier=cap; }`

findings.title: hypergrowth-quality-class derives ttmRev from filtered revY via short-circuit fall-through
findings.description: `const ttmRev = H.metricValue(stock, 'revenueTTM') || (Number.isFinite(revY[0]) ? revY[0] : 0);` — `||` operator: if revenueTTM is 0 (a legitimate value for pre-revenue companies), falls through to revY[0]. revY is `_rawArr(...)` which preserves nulls. If revY[0] is null, the ternary gives 0. Then `isMaterial = ttmRev >= 100e6 || (mcap > 0 && ttmRev / mcap >= 0.005)` — both conditions fail → not material → LOW_BASE_EFFECT. That's actually OK (correct behavior for pre-revenue). But for a company where revenueTTM is missing (null, not 0), `null || ternary` → ternary executes — if revY[0] is a valid number, ttmRev=that number. So the fallback works. The risk is when revenueTTM is set to 0 erroneously by the cache while annualRev shows real data — ttmRev becomes 0 (revenueTTM=0 is falsy in ||). Operator precedence with explicit null check would be safer.
findings.mechanism: `||` treats 0 as falsy — a stock with explicit revenueTTM=0 (set by cache or a stale snapshot) falls back to annual data. Result is usually correct, but the semantic is 'missing vs zero' ambiguity.
findings.reproducer: metrics.revenueTTM = {value: 0}, annual.annualRev[0] = {value: 1e9}. ttmRev becomes 1e9 (from fallback), not 0 → isMaterial=true → classification proceeds normally despite revenueTTM=0 being a flag.
findings.impact: Discrepancy between the explicit metrics.revenueTTM signal (cache says 0) and the annual-derived value. Other methods (q-spike-dataguard line 120 has identical pattern) propagate the same issue. Could mask cases where the cache wants to flag a stock as zero-TTM-revenue.
findings.suggested_fix: Use explicit null check: `let ttmRev = H.metricValue(stock, 'revenueTTM'); if (ttmRev == null) ttmRev = Number.isFinite(revY[0]) ? revY[0] : 0;`

findings.title: strategy-modes acceptValues fall-back uses r.components.state or r.components.trend by truthiness
findings.description: `const componentVal = r.components.state != null ? r.components.state : r.components.trend;` — uses .state preferentially. If a method has both .state and .trend in its components, .state wins. profitability-state emits .state. profitability-trend emits .trend. Different methods, no overlap. But if a future method emits both (or one emits a state with value `''` empty string), the logic could pick the wrong field. The `!= null` check correctly distinguishes from undefined, but empty string would be considered defined.
findings.mechanism: Implicit assumption that exactly one of state/trend is present per method. Brittle to future method authors who might use both names.
findings.reproducer: A hypothetical new method emits components: { state: '', trend: 'IMPROVING' }. componentVal becomes '' (empty string). acceptValues.includes('') → false → status='fail'. Reality wanted to check 'trend' but state took precedence.
findings.impact: Currently no method emits both. Forward-compatibility risk; a hidden coupling between mode-config and method internals.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the acceptValues check explicit about which component to read: `acceptComponentKey: 'state'` in mode config, then `componentVal = r.components[check.acceptComponentKey]`. Removes ambiguity.

findings.title: revenue-shock-guard filters quarters with _arr destroying positional alignment for median/MAD
findings.description: `qrev = _arr(stock, 'timeseries.revenueQ')` filters out nulls. Then `window = qrev.slice(0, 8); const latest = window[0]; const prior = window.slice(1)`. The 'latest' is the latest non-null quarter, not necessarily the latest calendar quarter. When the most recent quarter is null (pending earnings release), latest becomes the prior quarter — and the 'prior' window is the 7 quarters before that. The z-score is then computed against an incorrect baseline. For a fresh snapshot where Q0 is null but Q1 exists, the method labels the Q1 value as 'latest' and compares to Q2-Q8 — effectively masking the most recent period.
findings.mechanism: Filter-then-index treats nulls as transparent. The 'latest' in chronological time is silently replaced by the latest non-null.
findings.reproducer: timeseries.revenueQ = [null, {value:10e9}, {value:8e9}, {value:7e9}, {value:7e9}, {value:6e9}, {value:6e9}, {value:5e9}, {value:5e9}]. After _arr: [10,8,7,7,6,6,5,5] (in B). window.slice(0,8) = [10,8,7,7,6,6,5,5]. latest=10, prior median ~6.5, MAD ~1. z = (10-6.5)/(1.4826*1) = 2.36. Looks fine. But the REAL latest (Q0) is missing — what was published was Q1=10B. The shock-guard check is essentially 'is Q1 anomalous vs Q2-Q8' rather than the intended 'is Q0 anomalous vs Q1-Q7'.
findings.impact: Shock-guard misses or false-fires near earnings release windows when Q0 is pending. The method's intended semantics ('latest quarter shock') is silently re-anchored to the last reported quarter.
findings.suggested_fix: Use raw array for positional alignment. Either: require qRev[0] to be finite or return incomputable. Or explicitly take the latest finite value but tag the components with which calendar quarter it actually represents.
